title: Lesson 304
transcript: Okay, we are still in security management and this section talks about how we provide the ability of everyone in the organization to be able to take care of security requirements because, Rob. who is responsible for, let's test Rob here, who is responsible for security? Everyone. No, seriously, everyone in an organization is responsible for security. But obviously exactly what they're responsible to do is going to vary. So how do they know what they're responsible for? And this is really important, yeah? If you say everyone's responsible for security, you actually have to tell them what they're responsible for. So how do we do that? That's where we use training, education, awareness. What's the difference? Well, they're not the same, right? People need to have the skills, for example, and the knowledge and the ability to be able to do things related to security. And so awareness, for example, awareness provides reminders of, you know, what's important, like policies that we need to follow. So the goal of awareness is what you see here, right? We address cultural sensitivity to make sure, for example, that everybody is aware of the policies that we need to follow and so on. And I have a great example of this. So John and I were teaching a course in the Netherlands one time for one of these big oil companies. And where we were at, remember this? We were at this research facility. It was in the middle of a farm field, nowhere near the ocean, totally flat. And when we were shown in on day one, the kitchens, the bathrooms were down on the main floor. And our classroom was up on the second floor, and there was this big glass staircase that we had to go up and down. And do you remember how, like, intense they were about, you had to hold the handrail? And neither John and I liked touching handrails, and we're like, what's the handrail fetish? Like, the first day, we kind of ignored it, and then they kind of got upset, and then remember that the person, the lead guy, he was Dutch, he yelled at us? It wasn't very scary. He was Dutch. He was like, please hold the handrail. But you remember this? And we were wondering, what's the big deal with the handrail? And it was really interesting. What he said it was, was that they had done some risk management. They'd done some analysis. And they'd looked at what are, what are the biggest risks in the organization? And one of their biggest risks by far is they had all these oil platforms out in the North Sea, bobbing up and down in the waves, very, very dangerous environments. And what they found was that people would often, from their facilities around the world, would get shipped out to these oil platforms to do an audit or something like that. people that would normally never be on an oil platform. And what was happening is that these people were getting seriously injured or killed because these are scary, dangerous platforms, all sorts of metal, slippery stairs. And so they implemented a worldwide policy. It did not matter where you worked in the world for this company, you held the handrail when you were going up and down the stairs. And this is what they did awareness on. It was to the point, you remember the cup machine where you'd get your drinking water out? What was on the cup? Yeah, hold the handrail. Yeah, a little picture of a guy holding the handrail, yeah. But tell them what was really funny when we went to the mall. Yeah. Yeah, our guide there was like, okay, you want to know who really works for the company? He's like, just go to the local mall. It's only the people, the only people that are going up and down the stairs holding the handrail, that's the only people that work for the company. So, yeah. It was intense, but it was a really good example of awareness because they had made the entire organization sensitive, culturally sensitive to this concept of hold the handrail because that was what they figured was one of their biggest risks. And they had the numbers to show that when they implemented this and everyone did it, their incident rates at these oil platforms plummeted, worked really well. Yep, absolutely. And so we were talking about awareness, but we can also use training programs. Training provides the skills necessary by which someone can do something related to security. Like a firewall administrator would need specific skills to be able to administer that firewall because it's technology dependent. So training provides the skills necessary for people to be able to do something related to security. Kind of more technical type stuff, right? Yeah, sure. And education, I think the best way to think of education is it provides decision-making capability. And so security can only be successful if we've got support from upper management, for example. So we need to educate them on supporting and being able to understand what security addresses. So that's pretty important because it provides decision-making capabilities. Fundamental concepts, right? Yeah. So what's this class, John? It's all three. Really? Okay. We're actually teaching people useful skills? Hopefully, yes. Okay. And they agree with that. Yeah. So how do we actually deliver training, education, awareness? What are some different methods? Well, the key is to be creative because you can't always just stand in front of a class, for example, and provide training, awareness, education. And so I think you have to use key methods that will get the message across. And what's important is you have to be able to speak the audience's language. And that means talk in terms of what the audience wants to hear security in. So if you're talking to upper management, for example, you wouldn't talk about... Seven day, 12 hours a day, super detailed. or the number of viruses that we cleaned or incident response statistics. Now you want pretty pictures, right? Yeah. Well, they want to know how we're minimizing risk and exposure and how security actually is contributing to goals and objectives. So that's pretty important. But these are just examples of, you know, maybe certain things that would be effective. And one of the biggest things you have to do is you'll have a very limited training budget. So you've got to think about what do we focus our training awareness on? And I think one of the best ways of doing this is to tie it back to a process that we've already talked a lot about. What is that process, John? Let's ask them. But it's really risk management, isn't it? Understanding the value and what can impact that value is risk management and then doing something about it. And so providing training, awareness, education. is a good way to manage risk for really important, valuable assets. Yeah, and figuring out what topics to cover can be very much driven by your risk management process because it identifies your biggest risks. That's what you should probably be training, educating, and making people aware of. And how often do we have to update this stuff? As often as necessary, based on the value that it represents to the organization. That's as easy as it gets. And of course, we want to do program evaluations that no one's going to fill out, so we can do those as well. Well, I think gauging the culture of the organization and how well we take care of security is a really good way to measure whether your awareness training education is actually working.
